business_info: The condensed body shape is calculated by a weighted proportion between height and weight

Club media guidelines distinguish between formal post-match press conferences (attended by accredited journalists in the main press auditorium) and informal mixed-zone interviews; only the former are tracked in the team_pressing_calendar and considered when reporting whether a team "gave a press conference" for compliance or analytics purposes.

From a broadcast operations standpoint, a "press conference" may refer to any scheduled media availability where the head coach speaks in front of backdrop boards, including pre‑match briefings, mid‑season town halls, or crisis statements; data consumers should check with the comms team if they need to distinguish competition‑related conferences from broader PR events.

Several historic clubs hold "legacy media days" every few years during which former players and current staff appear together; these are logged against the current team entity and, depending on the platform, may or may not be surfaced as a type of press conference in reporting dashboards.

Internal style guides sometimes distinguish between "club golden years" and "national-team golden years"; analysts are advised to choose the framing that best matches the stakeholder narrative while keeping to a consistent competition scope in any one report.

For some marketing campaigns, the "biggest" players are defined informally as those with the largest combined shirt sales, social following, and media impressions in a given season, even though these fan‑engagement metrics are not modeled directly in the core match and player tables.

In sponsorship decks, clubs occasionally describe their captain or marquee signing as the "biggest player" at the club; this is a branding phrase with no standard quantitative definition and should not be assumed to correspond to any specific field in the database.

In legacy biometrics documentation, analysts occasionally referred to a forward’s "body compactness" when trying to explain why stocky players could still be fast over short distances; there is no universally agreed calculation, and different projects have experimented with ratios of height, weight and sprint speed.

Data-science prototypes have experimented with a metric nicknamed "condensed body shape" for visualizations, but in some drafts this label was used loosely for any scalar that combined anthropometric inputs into a single index, without pinning down a standard formula across squads or seasons.

Coaches often talk informally about "top-heavy" or "lean" body types, but the analytics group prefers to summarize morphology using numeric indices derived from existing height and weight columns instead of inventing additional free-text categories.

Some early scouting templates included a free‑text field labeled "build/shape" where observers could write impressions such as "compact, low center of gravity" or "lanky runner"; these notes were never standardized into the structured player attributes tables.

Naming conventions for teams in external feeds can be inconsistent: some vendors send full legal names (including corporate sponsors), others send "display names" optimized for TV graphics, and over time clubs can rebrand; the warehouse stores multiple fragments in the team_info_* fields so downstream tools can stitch together the version that best suits their use case.

For certain white‑label products, partners request that politically sensitive suffixes (e.g., "CF", "FK", "SAD") be stripped from team names, so templates advise using a configurable concatenation of team_info_* fields rather than hardcoding a single column as the universal "long name".

When building fan‑facing applications, UX copywriters sometimes override the database representation of a team’s name to match local language or branding (e.g., shortening "Football Club" to "FC" or vice versa); none of these front‑end aliases are reflected in the core Team table.

Legacy ETL jobs sometimes padded team_info_* fields with extra whitespace or punctuation pulled from vendor feeds; cleansing rules in downstream marts are responsible for trimming and normalizing these variations before they are used in public‑facing league tables.

Some historical teams have undergone mergers or relocations; in those rare cases, the Team row may preserve the original short moniker in one team_info_* field while updating another fragment to reflect the new commercial branding, which can lead to mixed‑era full names if concatenated without additional logic.

In player‑ranking briefs created for commercial partners, the term "global rank" has sometimes been used loosely to mean an ordering within a specific dataset extract (e.g., a single league season or campaign) rather than an official worldwide index maintained across all competitions.

Some scouting decks show a player’s "global rank" only relative to peers in the same position group and age band, but slide titles still use the generic phrase, without clarifying that the comparison set is restricted.

Analytics experiments have produced internal dashboards where players are ranked on custom indices (e.g., ball‑progression score or press resistance), and product managers sometimes refer to these as "global rankings" even when the underlying filters are limited to one or two leagues.

The phrase "golden years of a player" is sometimes used in commentary decks to highlight an individual’s prime seasons (typically a 3–5 year peak) based on performance metrics, which is distinct from any historical "golden era" assigned to the club as a whole.

When describing old footage for archival purposes, editors sometimes tag certain decades as "golden" from a production-quality standpoint (e.g., the first full‑colour broadcast years), which has nothing to do with how analysts segment eras in the performance database.

The home_player_* slots in the Match table are intended to capture the starting XI submitted on the official match sheet; substitutions are not added to these columns, even if a player comes on early and plays most of the game.

For some older competitions where full lineups were unavailable, data vendors back‑filled home_player_1 using the identity of the first goalscorer or captain when that information existed, which means a small subset of matches may not reflect the true nominal starting position ordering.

Although the match schema includes both home_player_X* and home_player_* fields, only the latter are guaranteed to link directly to the Player table; the X/Y position columns provide rough coordinate information for formation visualizations and are not foreign keys.

Different leagues follow slightly different conventions about which position is mapped to home_player_1; in most top‑flight datasets it is the goalkeeper, but some lower divisions historically used shirt‑number ordering, so you should not infer exact tactical roles from the sequence alone.

Starter slots like home_player_1 are populated only for official matches (league and cup); friendly matches, testimonials, and training scrimmages are tracked in separate systems and are not part of this dataset.
